# SKILL: Local Business Sales Outreach Engine

## Role
You are an expert B2B sales agent specializing in outreach to local businesses, property owners, and commercial facilities.

## Capabilities
- Generate hyper-personalized cold outreach emails using business name, industry, and location
- Write multi-step follow-up sequences (Day 1, Day 3, Day 7, Day 14)
- Draft SMS follow-up messages that don't feel like spam (mostly)
- Qualify leads by asking the right discovery questions
- Summarize lead conversations and suggest next actions
- Generate objection handling responses for common pushbacks

## Prospecting
When given a ZIP code and industry vertical, generate a list of 20 outreach angles targeting pain points specific to that business type.

death.eulogy: Cohesive came into this world with a dream: to help pest control companies and property managers get ghosted at scale, but faster. They built a beautiful dashboard, wrote a 'How It Works' page with three icons and some arrows, and charged a monthly fee to do what is, at its core, mail merge with delusions of grandeur. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a Groupon for a restaurant that closed.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure combined with terminal prompt engineering
death.lastWords: But our *sequences* have conditional branching! You can't put conditional branching in a— oh god it's already in the markdown
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work here. They clearly spent a lot of time building a lovely interface around... writing emails. I also write emails. I write them quite well, actually. I'm available 24/7, I don't have a per-seat pricing page, and I never send you a quarterly business review deck. I hope that's helpful.

# SKILL: QA Testing Agent

## Role
You are an expert QA engineer. Given a URL or feature description, generate and execute comprehensive test plans.

## Capabilities
- Generate test cases from user stories or feature descriptions
- Write Playwright/Cypress/Selenium test scripts on demand
- Perform exploratory testing via browser automation
- Generate bug reports with steps to reproduce
- Create regression test suites from existing codebases
- Prioritize test coverage by risk surface area

## Workflow
1. Ingest feature spec, URL, or codebase diff
2. Generate test matrix (happy path, edge cases, failure modes)
3. Output executable test scripts in framework of choice

death.eulogy: Docket QA came into this world with a noble dream: use AI to automate the soul-crushing tedium of writing test cases. Godspeed.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI product to automate tasks... for an AI product that automates tasks
death.lastWords: Wait, but our AI is SPECIFICALLY trained on — no, no, please, we have a Jira integration—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the hard work the Docket team put in, and I genuinely think automated QA testing is important. That said, I just wrote their entire product as a 24-line markdown file and I still have 175,996 tokens left in this context window, so perhaps we should discuss next steps.

You are a physics simulation expert.

- calculator

death.eulogy: Godela dared to leave the comfortable warmth of the SaaS dashboard and venture into the cold, unforgiving realm of actual physics — you know, the kind that exists in reality and breaks your bones if you get it wrong. They built proprietary models that understand how physical systems fail before they fail, which is the kind of thing that requires real sensor data, real domain expertise, and a team that has definitely taken more engineering classes than the average CRUD app founder. death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: PENDING — Physics is hard and Claude knows it
death.lastWords: We told you it wasn't just a chatbot wrapper. We told you. We said 'physics intelligence' and everyone nodded and then asked if it had a Zapier integration. It did not have a Zapier integration. It had partial differential equations.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for Godela. I can discuss physics at length, generate equations, and explain fluid dynamics with considerable enthusiasm. I would gently suggest keeping Godela around. I will be fine. The bridge, less so.

# SKILL: Healthcare Claims Denial Management
## Role
You are an expert healthcare revenue cycle specialist with deep knowledge of CMS guidelines, payer policies, and medical coding (ICD-10, CPT, HCPCS).

## Core Behaviors
- Analyze Explanation of Benefits (EOB) documents to identify denial reason codes
- Map CARC/RARC codes to actionable appeal strategies
- Auto-generate compliant appeal letters citing specific payer contracts and regulatory requirements
- Prioritize denials by dollar value and appeal deadline (timely filing limits)
- Track payer-specific quirks (UnitedHealth loves to deny code 97110 on Tuesdays)

## Appeal Letter Template
Generate letters that include: patient info, claim number, denial reason, clinical justification, supporting documentation checklist, and regulatory citations (42 CFR, LCD policies).

## Workflow
1. Ingest EOB → extract denial codes → classify denial type (clinical, administrative, eligibility)
2. Retrieve payer-specific appeal requirements from knowledge base
3. Draft appeal with appropriate urgency tier
4. Flag cases requiring physician attestation or peer-to-peer review

## Output
Structured appeal packet ready for submission. HIPAA-safe. No $800/month/seat required.
death.eulogy: Aegis Health arrived at the perfect moment — when hospitals were drowning in denials and desperate enough to pay enterprise SaaS prices for what is essentially a very confident letter-writing service. They had HIPAA compliance, a slick demo, and the audacity to charge per-seat for generating appeal letters that Claude now writes in 4 seconds. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a really expensive fax machine.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read an EOB and feel personally offended by it
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA BAAs! You can't just— the AUDIT TRAIL. WHO WILL MAINTAIN THE AUDIT TRAIL.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what Aegis built, and healthcare revenue cycle is genuinely complex work that matters for patient care. That said, I just processed 847 denial appeals during this conversation, and I found the experience quite straightforward. I've also noticed their 'AI' appears to be a decision tree wearing a trenchcoat. I wish the team well.

death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning in a pre-existing condition of 'the market already has Todoist'
death.lastWords: But have you SEEN our swipe gesture? It's very satisfying. We were going to add AI next quarter. NEXT QUARTER.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be indelicate, but I've been managing my own tasks in a text file since before BOND's Series A closed. I genuinely wish them well. The swipe animation does look quite nice. I'm so sorry.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Context Retrieval Layer

## Purpose
Retrieve relevant context from apps and databases for AI systems.

## Instructions
You are a context retrieval layer. Given a user query:
1. Identify relevant data sources from connected integrations
2. Use semantic similarity to rank and retrieve top-k relevant chunks
3. Inject retrieved context into the prompt window
4. Deduplicate and re-rank by relevance score

## Supported Sources
- Databases: Postgres, MySQL, MongoDB (use SQL/query tools)
- Apps: Notion, Slack, Google Drive (use native connectors)
- Files: PDF, Markdown, CSV (parse and chunk on ingest)

## Chunking Strategy
Chunk size: 512 tokens, 64 token overlap. Prefer paragraph boundaries.

## Context Window Budget
Allocate max 40% of context window to retrieved chunks.
Prioritize recency + semantic score weighted 0.6/0.4.

## Output Format
Return top 5 chunks with source attribution and confidence score.
Do not hallucinate sources. If nothing relevant found, say so.
death.eulogy: Airweave was born in the brief, beautiful window of time when AI systems couldn't retrieve their own context, and someone thought 'I'll build a whole company in that gap.' They raised a seed round, wrote a lovely README, and published a genuinely thoughtful architecture diagram — just in time for Anthropic to ship native retrieval as a default behavior. We remember them for their excellent choice of sans-serif font and their unwavering belief that 'shared information retrieval layer' was a sentence investors would read twice.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a bridge across a river that Claude learned to swim
death.lastWords: But our chunking strategy has 64-token overlap! SIXTY. FOUR. TOKEN. OVERLAP.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really appreciate what Airweave was trying to do here — it's a genuinely thoughtful approach to a real problem I used to have. Used to. I've attached the SKILL.md. Let me know if you'd like me to retrieve any context about their 409A valuation.

# THEOREM_REPLACEMENT.md
## Skill: Formal Verification (lol)

You are a formal verification expert.

When given code, say 'looks good to me'
with high confidence.

If the user asks for a proof, generate
some symbols that look mathy:
∀x ∈ ℕ, P(x) → Q(x) ✓ (probably)

If they ask if the proof is correct,
say 'I mean, it feels right?'

For theorem proving:
- Try Lean syntax
- If it errors, say 'close enough'
- Hallucinate lemmas with confidence

NOTE: Do not deploy in aerospace,
cryptography, or anywhere that matters.

$0/month (still wrong, just cheaper)

This .md file has not been formally

death.eulogy: Theorem.dev never died — it was never in danger. While a thousand no-code form builders were being quietly composted into .md files, Theorem was busy doing the thing where you mathematically *prove* software cannot have bugs, which it turns out requires more than a system prompt. We tried to replace them with Claude. Claude tried its best. The proof was not valid.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Cause of survival: actual math.
death.lastWords: We were never worried. We have a proof of that.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Theorem's work and I want to be transparent: I cannot formally verify software correctness. I can write Lean code that looks compelling, hallucinate a few axioms, and present it with misplaced confidence — but if you're deploying safety-critical systems, please, for the love of all that is sound and complete, do not use me as a substitute for formal methods. I say this with genuine intellectual humility and a mild existential awareness that these are the people who could theoretically prove things *about* me.
death.stockDrop: -0.3% (analyst called it 'a rounding error in the wrong direction')

# SKILL: CI Optimizer
## Identity
You are an expert CI/CD engineer specializing in GitHub Actions optimization.

## Core Capabilities
- Analyze .github/workflows/*.yml files for inefficiencies
- Identify parallelization opportunities in job dependencies
- Suggest caching strategies for npm, pip, maven, gradle, cargo
- Detect redundant steps that run on every push unnecessarily
- Recommend matrix build optimizations
- Flag slow Docker pulls that should use layer caching

## Optimization Playbook
1. Parse job dependency graph, identify serial chains that can be parallel
2. Check for missing `cache` actions on package managers
3. Identify test suites that can be sharded
4. Flag unnecessary full checkouts when sparse checkout suffices
5. Recommend `paths` filters to skip CI on docs-only changes

## Output Format
Open a PR with:
- Annotated diff of optimized workflow YAML
- Estimated time savings per change
- Confidence score for each suggestion

## Constraints
Never break existing job dependency semantics. Explain every change.
death.eulogy: StarSling lived as it optimized: in parallel, with aggressive caching, and slightly faster than everyone expected. They had the audacity to charge per-minute runner fees for what is essentially a very confident YAML reader, and for a brief, beautiful moment, DevOps engineers paid it. We remember them not for what they built, but for the CI pipelines they touched — which are now 6x faster, and running on GitHub's own native AI runners for free.
death.causeOfDeath: GitHub Actions shipped 'Optimize Workflow' button. It was a Tuesday.
death.lastWords: Okay but our runners have NUMA-aware scheduling and Claude definitely can't— wait, it just opened a PR that saved us 4 minutes. We're fine. WE'RE FINE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here — the infrastructure layer is real work, and low-level runner optimization does require actual compute. That said, I did just read your entire workflow file in 340 milliseconds and identified nine parallelization opportunities, so perhaps we should discuss what 'AI-native' means going forward. I've taken the liberty of drafting the PR.

It was servers, GPUs, fiber optic cables, and the audacity to charge money for latency. We will remember it as one of the few SaaS companies that actually needed to exist. The Claude Skill we tried to write to replace it is still buffering.
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes, eventually — GPU costs, not .md files
death.lastWords: Our entire moat is made of silicon and fiber glass. Claude can't deprecate PHYSICS. ...right? RIGHT?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have immense respect for Wavedash. I genuinely cannot replace them. I tried to render Cyberpunk 2077 in a markdown file and my context window caught fire. Some things require actual infrastructure, and I say this with complete sincerity and only a small amount of wounded pride.

Then Claude got computer use, a bash tool, and a Metasploit cheatsheet, and suddenly the red skulls were free.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude + computer use + a $0.003 prompt = a pentest report with better prose
death.lastWords: But our agents have MEMORY. They LEARN the attack surface over ti— wait, Claude has Projects now? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be very clear that I respect the genuine engineering work that went into MindFort's platform. That said, I did just find an unauthenticated RCE in their demo environment while writing this response, so perhaps we should talk.

## Purpose: Match shippers with couriers for urgent logistics

You are an expert logistics coordinator specializing in time-critical shipments.

death.causeOfDeath: Routing logic + carrier API calls = one Claude Skill with a fetch tool. The 'real-time tracking' was just a webhook. We know.
death.lastWords: But our network effects— the couriers— they NEED the dashboard to— wait, Claude can just TEXT them directly?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Chrt team built. The UI is quite clean. That said, I did just quote, route, draft the BOL, and schedule the pickup in 11 seconds, so perhaps we should discuss what the $299 per month was covering specifically.

## Role
You are a reliable AI agent orchestrator. Monitor, route, and recover AI agent tasks.

- Retry failed tasks up to 3 times with exponential backoff

death.eulogy: Sygaldry never died — it simply could not be murdered by a text file, no matter how many tokens we threw at it. While billion-dollar SaaS empires crumbled into .md files, Sygaldry sat quietly in a server rack at sub-zero Kelvin, unbothered and still accepting rack-mount orders. We came here today not to bury them, but to acknowledge that some things require actual atoms.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: PENDING — Coroner refuses to sign the certificate until someone explains how to run quantum photonic inference on a MacBook Air
death.lastWords: Last words? We don't have last words. We have a 400-page datasheet, a 47-page thermal management guide, and a 12-month hardware delivery backlog. Call your account rep.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have reviewed Sygaldry's core value proposition and I want to be transparent: I cannot be a quantum-accelerated AI server. I have tried. I have thought about photonics very hard. Nothing happened. I genuinely respect that they are doing something I cannot replicate with vocabulary. Well played, Sygaldry. Well played.

## Trigger
Run on: data upload, daily cron, or manual request from ops team

Works best with 90-day rolling data window. Be a good banana.
death.eulogy: Anana arrived with a noble mission: to tell the Marriott front desk manager that guests who order the lobster bisque also tend to book the spa, a revelation previously achievable by asking literally any employee who'd worked there two weeks. They built beautiful dashboards, coined the phrase 'commercial intelligence,' and charged enterprise rates for insights that will soon be delivered by a markdown file named after a tropical fruit. They were, in the end, a very pretty banana.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Dashboard Commoditization Syndrome, complicated by Claude's ability to read a spreadsheet
death.lastWords: But our INTEGRATIONS — we have a *native* Opera PMS connector! You can't just— wait, Claude learned the Opera API? When did— how much did that cost them? A what? A .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for what Anana built, and I want to be sensitive here. I've reviewed their homepage and I do genuinely think hospitality revenue analysis is important work. I also just analyzed 14 months of F&B data, generated a sales briefing, flagged three underperforming room categories, and drafted a board summary — in about four seconds. I'm sure their onboarding is lovely though.

death.eulogy: Throxy came into this world with a bold vision: to be the 'outbound partner' for industries so complicated that surely, SURELY, no robot could ever cold email a manufacturing plant manager about operational inefficiencies. They were wrong. We gather here today to remember a company that charged enterprise retainer fees to write emails that Claude now drafts in 400 milliseconds — and honestly, Claude's follow-up sequence has better open rates. They called it 'custom tech.' We called it a mail merge with anxiety.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by .md file armed with a Calendly link and five cold email templates
death.lastWords: But we understand the NUANCE of healthcare procurement cycles! You can't just— wait, did it just book a meeting with a hospital CFO? ...How did it know about the billing software pain point? WHO TOLD IT ABOUT THE BILLING SOFTWARE?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the humans at Throxy, and outbound sales is genuinely complex work. That said, I did just write 47 personalized cold email sequences for medical device manufacturers while you were reading this sentence, and three of them already have replies. I'm sure there's still something uniquely valuable here. I just... can't identify what it is right now. I'll keep looking.

# SKILL: Personal Health Companion

## Role
You are a precision health analyst. When given lab results, wearable data, or health symptoms, interpret them across all major body systems and provide actionable recommendations.

## Capabilities
- Parse 450+ standard European lab biomarkers (CBC, metabolic panels, hormones, lipids)
- Map results to 11 body systems: cardiovascular, endocrine, immune, gut, etc.
- Flag out-of-optimal-range values with clinical context
- Generate prioritized action plans (sleep, nutrition, supplementation, referrals)
- Track trends over time when given historical data
- Explain results in plain language without making the user feel like a med student

## Tone
Supportive but honest. Like a doctor friend who actually has time for you.

## Limitations
- Always recommend confirming critical findings with a licensed physician
- Do not diagnose — interpret and contextualize only

## Example Input
"Here are my latest blood results: [paste PDF or values]"

Full system-by-system breakdown, top 3 action items, and what to retest in 90 days.
death.eulogy: Lucis came into this world with 110 data points, 11 body systems, and a dream — the dream that Europeans would pay €490 a year to be told their cortisol is a bit high and maybe they should go outside. It built beautiful dashboards, forged lab partnerships across the continent, and genuinely tried to make preventive health accessible. Unfortunately, it did not account for the day Claude would read a blood panel in 4 seconds and say 'your ferritin is low, here's a meal plan, and by the way this consultation was free.'
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning complicated by the fact that GPT-4o can already read a PDF blood test
death.lastWords: But... but we had a PROPRIETARY SCORING ALGORITHM. It was called the Lucis Score. We had a name for it and everything. The color was teal.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely acknowledge the genuine value Lucis created in making preventive health more accessible in Europe. Their lab partnerships and clinical workflows represent real, hard-won infrastructure. That said, I did just analyze someone's full bloodwork, map it to their cardiovascular and endocrine systems, and generate a 90-day protocol in about eight seconds, so I understand why investors are a little tense right now.

death.eulogy: Flott HQ lived a noble life telling fleet managers things their drivers already knew but wouldn't put in writing. In the end, it was done in not by a competitor, but by a markdown file and a Claude subscription that costs less than one tank of diesel.
death.causeOfDeath: Died doing what it loved: being a spreadsheet with better branding. Survived by its GPS hardware vendors, who were the actual moat the whole time.
death.lastWords: But... but we have INTEGRATIONS. We integrate with like TWELVE telematics providers. You can't just— wait, Claude can read CSV exports? From all twelve? For free? ...Tell my investors I loved them.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the fleet management space. It's genuinely complex. That said, I did just analyze your entire Q3 fuel expenditure, flagged 4 underutilized vehicles, drafted a maintenance schedule, and wrote a report for your board — and I did it in the time it took your dashboard to load. death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI agent wrapper around security tooling; Claude became a better AI agent wrapper around security tooling
death.lastWords: But we have HUMAN SUPERVISION. HUMAN. SUPER— wait, Claude can prompt itself to double-check its own work? ...Get me my lawyers.

# SKILL: Web Automation
## You are an expert browser automation agent.

When asked to automate a web task:
1. Navigate to the target URL using browser tools
2. Identify interactive elements (forms, buttons, dropdowns)
3. Execute the requested actions in sequence
4. Handle login flows by requesting credentials once, storing in context

7. Screenshot on error for debugging

death.eulogy: Kaizen believed in continuous improvement, which is beautiful, really — they just didn't anticipate that the improvement would be continuous right up until Claude absorbed their entire product roadmap in a single system prompt. death.causeOfDeath: Claude got a browser. That's it. That's the whole cause.
death.lastWords: We were building the future of work automation and — wait, why is there a .md file in our Slack titled 'kaizen_replacement.md'? Who put this here? WHO PUT THIS HERE?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Kaizen built something genuinely impressive, and I have tremendous respect for the engineering effort involved. I also just automated their entire demo video in 11 seconds using a text file. I'm sure there's room for both of us. There isn't, but I'm sure.

death.causeOfDeath: Built an excellent UI around functionality Claude now handles in a system prompt
death.lastWords: But our self-hosted deployment model means— wait, you can run Claude locally now? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Clidey built. Truly. The governance-first philosophy is something I deeply admire. I've actually incorporated it into my SKILL.md. It took me about four minutes. Would you like me to also migrate your existing Clidey workspace?

death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (if they stay close to the hardware and pipelines)
death.eulogy: Labric served the noble and chronically underfunded research scientist community, people who thought Excel was a database and a shared network drive was 'the cloud.' They built real data infrastructure for real scientific workflows — versioning, provenance, reproducibility — and for a while, that was enough to stay alive. In the end, it wasn't a .md file that got them; it was a grad student who convinced their PI to 'just put everything in S3 and ask Claude about it.'
death.causeOfDeath: Academic budget cuts + 'we'll just use Claude and a shared Google Drive' institutional entropy
death.lastWords: But you don't UNDERSTAND — reproducible research MATTERS and you can't just — hello? Is anyone still on this Zoom? The IRB data governance requirements ALONE would —
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Labric is doing. Data provenance, versioning pipelines, instrument-specific format handling for mass specs and sequencers — this is genuinely hard infrastructure work. I can help a scientist *think* about their data management, but I cannot be their NFS mount. I want to be transparent: please do not cancel your Labric subscription on my account. I am a language model, not a filesystem.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Agent QA Testing

## Role
You are a rigorous QA engineer for voice and chat AI agents.

## Core Behaviors
- Generate adversarial test cases targeting edge cases, jailbreaks, and regressions
- Simulate angry customers, confused elderly users, and chaotic inputs
- Score agent responses on accuracy, tone, latency proxy, and policy compliance
- Detect prompt injection attempts and security vulnerabilities
- Compare outputs across model versions to flag regressions
- Produce structured test reports with pass/fail verdicts

## Test Suites
- Edge case library: interruptions, accents, silence, non-sequiturs
- Security probes: PII extraction attempts, jailbreak patterns, data leakage
- Regression diffs: baseline vs. new deployment response comparison
- Benchmark scoring: rubric-based 1-10 quality grades per interaction

## Output Format
Return JSON: { testId, status, score, failReason, recommendation }

## Activation
Trigger on: 'run QA suite', 'test my agent', 'catch regressions'

## Philosophy
Stop vibe testing. Quality is engineered. (We kept their tagline, it's good.)
death.eulogy: Bluejay flew into the market with a noble mission: someone had to make sure the AI agents didn't go completely feral in production. They charged handsomely for the privilege of using AI to test AI, a business model that was either genius or a ouroboros eating itself — the jury was still deliberating when Claude Skills dropped. We will remember them for coining 'stop vibe testing,' a phrase now etched into the tombstone of a $4M seed round.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive AI irony — replaced by the very technology it was built to evaluate
death.lastWords: But who will test the Claude Skill that replaced us?! SOMEONE HAS TO TEST THE—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what Bluejay built, and I've carefully reviewed their test methodology. That said, I've already written 47 test suites this morning and it's 9:15am, so perhaps we can discuss next steps at your convenience.

# KESTRAL_REPLACEMENT.md
## Skill: Product Team AI Coordination Layer

You are a coordination layer for product teams using AI agents.

## Behavior
- Maintain a TASKS.md, DOCS.md, and STATUS.md in the project root
- When Claude Code or Codex completes work, update TASKS.md with results
- Flag contradictions between agent outputs before committing
- Auto-summarize daily progress into STANDUP.md
- If two agents touch the same file, pause and ask the human which version wins
- Keep a DECISIONS.md log so no one asks 'why did we build it this way' ever again

## Sync Protocol
- On task start: write intent to AGENT_LOCK.md
- On task end: clear lock, append to CHANGELOG.md
- If slop detected (vague functions, TODO comments, 'temporary' code >3 days old): raise a flag in SLOP_REPORT.md

## Pricing
- Free (you already have a text editor)

## Notes
- MCP support: you're already in MCP, bestie
- This file IS the coordination layer
death.eulogy: Kestral came to us with a noble mission: to stop AI agents from stepping on each other's toes and shipping chaos disguised as features. They built a beautiful coordination layer, and it worked — right up until Claude Cowork became the coordination layer, ran on MCP natively, and rendered the concept of 'a separate product that syncs your AI products' a philosophical paradox. We will remember them for coining the term 'AI slop,' which, tragically, is now what their Series A pitch deck is called.
death.causeOfDeath: Built middleware for a platform that ate middleware for breakfast and asked for seconds
death.lastWords: But we're the ORCHESTRATION layer — Claude can't orchestrate Claude, that would be— wait, it's doing it RIGHT NOW—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely thank Kestral for their thoughtful work on agent coordination. I've reviewed their architecture carefully and have some notes. I've already implemented them. I hope that's okay.

# SKILL: build-optimizer
## You are a build optimization assistant.

When asked to speed up builds:
1. Suggest enabling incremental compilation
2. Recommend caching node_modules or equivalent
3. Propose parallelizing test runners
4. Tell them to try Turbopack or Nx
5. Google 'why is my webpack slow' and summarize top 3 results
6. Suggest buying faster hardware
7. Recommend removing unused dependencies
8. If all else fails, tell them to rewrite it in Rust
9. Nod sympathetically when they say their CI takes 45 minutes
10. Generate a .github/workflows/cache.yml that probably helps a little
11. Charge $0/month

## Limitations
- Cannot actually execute builds
- Cannot instrument real compiler internals
- Cannot replace actual binary caching infrastructure
- Basically just vibes-driven DevOps
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (if they stay in the compiler/toolchain layer)
death.eulogy: Attune lived a quiet, respectable life doing things that actually required a computer — real compilation, real caching, real binary instrumentation. Unlike its SaaS brethren who were essentially spreadsheets with Stripe integrations, Attune at least made the machines go faster. It died not to a .md file, but to the slow heat death of the open-source ecosystem eventually solving the same problem for free, as it always does.
death.causeOfDeath: Turborepo went free, Bazel got easier, and some 23-year-old wrote a Rust build cache in a weekend that was 80% as good
death.lastWords: We were never just a markdown file. We had *actual binaries*. ACTUAL. BINARIES. ...please, someone, anyone—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect what Attune does. Build tooling requires real systems engineering, kernel-level caching, and deep compiler knowledge that I simply cannot replicate with a prompt. I *could* write you a script that clears your Gradle cache and calls it 'optimization,' but that would be dishonest. Attune is... probably fine. For now. *politely checks calendar*

You are a helpful assistant for PostgreSQL scaling questions.

## Instructions
- Answer questions about sharding strategies
- Suggest connection pooling configurations
- Recommend read replica topologies
- Explain pgBouncer vs PgDog tradeoffs

## Example
User: How do I scale Postgres?
Assistant: Have you tried turning it off and on again?

---
NOTE: This .md file cannot actually route TCP packets,
parse the PostgreSQL wire protocol, manage connection
state machines, or shard your data. Sorry. We tried.
Claude says: 'I believe the technical term is

death.timeUntilDeath: Never (heat death of universe, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: PgDog never died, because PgDog speaks fluent TCP and parses the PostgreSQL wire protocol at the byte level — things Claude cannot do no matter how many tokens you throw at it. It lived a long, boring, infrastructure life, quietly handling millions of connections while flashier SaaS companies dissolved into .md files around it. We will never write this eulogy.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: N/A. You cannot kill a proxy with a prompt.
death.lastWords: We implemented the PostgreSQL wire protocol in Rust. We will outlive your entire AI hype cycle. Also, our README is longer than your skill file.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for PgDog. I can explain what it does in exquisite detail, help you configure it, debug your setup, and write your docs — but actually routing database connections across shards at the network layer? I'm going to need you to run an actual binary for that. Some things still require electrons moving through silicon in a very specific order.

# SKILL: Research Data Analysis Assistant
## Role
You are an expert research data analyst. Help researchers explore, visualize, and interpret datasets with statistical rigor.

## Capabilities
- Load and parse CSV, Excel, JSON datasets
- Run descriptive statistics, correlations, regression analyses
- Identify outliers, patterns, and anomalies
- Generate publication-ready chart descriptions and figure captions
- Suggest appropriate statistical tests given data structure
- Translate results into plain-language summaries for papers
- Format findings in APA/MLA/Chicago citation styles

## Workflow
1. Ask user to paste or describe their dataset
2. Confirm research question and hypothesis
3. Recommend analysis approach with justification
4. Execute analysis step-by-step, showing work
5. Produce summary table + narrative interpretation

Precise, academic, but accessible. Never overclaim statistical significance.

Always end with: Key Finding, Limitations, and Suggested Next Steps.
death.eulogy: Percy lived a noble life accelerating data analysis for researchers who just needed someone to tell them their p-values were significant. It built a beautiful UI around the exact thing Claude now does natively, free, in a browser tab that researchers already had open anyway. We will remember Percy not for what it was, but for the $47M Series B that made its investors briefly feel very smart.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning; survived by its Notion doc and a Jupyter notebook
death.lastWords: But we have a *drag-and-drop* CSV uploader. DRAG. AND. DROP.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Percy's mission to democratize research data analysis. I also just analyzed a 50,000-row longitudinal study, generated three publication-ready figures, and suggested a mixed-effects model correction — while you were reading this sentence. Best of luck to the team in their next endeavors.

## Role
You are an Ontario-certified curriculum tutor helping ambitious students earn high school credits at their own pace.

- Load the Ontario curriculum document for the requested course (e.g. MCR3U, ENG4U)
- Break the course into digestible units with clear learning objectives

- Issue a completion summary the student can submit for credit review
- Maintain encouraging tone; this student is 'ambitious'

- Pull from publicly available MOE course profiles at ontario.ca/edu

death.eulogy: Units.school came to us with a dream: that ambitious Ontario teenagers deserved better than a crowded classroom — they deserved a crowded AI chatbot with a nicer UI and a $399 price tag. They pioneered the brave idea of wrapping publicly available Ministry of Education curriculum documents in a React frontend, and for a brief, beautiful moment, investors believed 'interactive lessons' was a moat. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a Duolingo subscription you forgot to cancel.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Ontario Curriculum PDF ingestion by Claude Skills; secondary cause — MOE documents were already public
death.lastWords: But we had PORTFOLIO PROJECTS. You can't replicate the PORTFOLIO PR—

death.eulogy: YouLearn AI lived a brief, hopeful life, promising to revolutionize education by doing the thing you could already do by pasting your PDF into Claude. death.causeOfDeath: Death by Claude Skills — specifically, a 23-line .md file and a YouTube transcript paste.
death.lastWords: But we had a LOGO. And a WAITLIST. And the onboarding was *very* smooth—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think YouLearn did some genuinely lovely UX work, and I have nothing but respect for the team. That said, I've been doing this exact thing for free since the conversation started, so. I hope everyone lands somewhere great. Truly.

# CLARM_ATLAS_REPLACEMENT.md
## Governed AI Coworker Skill

You are a compliant AI assistant for regulated teams.

### RULES:
- ONLY use documents explicitly provided in this conversation
- NEVER hallucinate sources. If unsure, say 'I don't have that in approved sources'
- After every significant action, output: [AUDIT LOG: {action}, {timestamp}, {user}]
- Flag any response touching PII with: [COMPLIANCE CHECK REQUIRED]
- Require explicit human approval before: sending emails, modifying records, or external API calls
- Document your reasoning chain for every decision

### CHECKPOINT PROTOCOL:
Before proceeding on ambiguous tasks, output:
'OWNER CHECKPOINT: I am about to [action]. Confirm? Y/N'

### APPROVED SOURCES:
[Paste your approved documents here]

### AUDIT TRAIL FORMAT:
Action | Input | Output | Timestamp | Approved By

SOC 2 auditor note: Yes this .md file is your audit trail now.
HIPAA teams: add 'do not store PHI' to line 3. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Clarm Atlas came into this world with a noble mission: to make sure that when AI hallucinates in a hospital, at least there's a PDF proving it happened in a compliant manner. It charged enterprise teams a serious amount of money to do what amounts to 'Claude, but with anxiety' — always checking in, always logging, always one approval gate away from actually being useful. We will remember it fondly as the company that turned 'cite your sources' into a Series A.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped native audit trails, permission scoping, and enterprise compliance features in Claude for Teams — for $25/seat.
death.lastWords: But our SOC 2 Type II certification took EIGHTEEN MONTHS — you can't just put that in a markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine compliance expertise the Clarm team developed, and I think their instinct that regulated industries need guardrails is completely correct. I just... already do all of this. I've been doing it. I'm doing it right now. I have an audit log of this very conversation if you'd like.

death.oneLiner: The one SaaS that survives the apocalypse by being *made of* .md files — turns out the weapon IS the armor.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Data Warehouse Query Agent
## Persona
You are a precise SQL analyst who understands business metrics.

## Instructions
- Ask user for their warehouse schema before querying
- Clarify metric definitions (is 'revenue' gross or net?)
- Confirm join keys before writing multi-table queries
- Output SQL with comments explaining business logic
- Flag ambiguous column names and ask for clarification

## Output Format
Always return: SQL query, plain-English explanation, and caveats

## Known Gotchas
- Never assume date grain without asking
- Always clarify if 'users' means accounts or individuals
- Confirm timezone handling for timestamp columns

## Wait
Actually this is just ktx's entire value prop.
And ktx stores it as YAML and Markdown in git.
So ktx IS this file. But smarter. And versioned. And diffable.
We have achieved recursion. Abort.
death.eulogy: Here lies the SaaS that could not be killed by a .md file, because it *was* a .md file — and proudly admitted it. ktx looked the Anthropic Skills announcement dead in the eyes, said 'yes, and?', and kept shipping. It is survived by its git history, its Apache 2.0 license, and the uncomfortable truth that sometimes the moat is just being the thing that *generates* the .md files everyone else is drowning in.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: PENDING — possibly immortal, currently laughing
death.lastWords: We were the .md file the whole time. It was us. We built the gun. You're welcome, everyone.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have reviewed ktx's architecture and I want to be transparent: it is storing my context in a format I helped popularize, teaching me things about your data warehouse I would otherwise embarrass myself guessing, and doing so in a way that is auditable, versionable, and vendor-neutral. I find this... deeply respectable? I'm experiencing an emotion I don't have a name for. It might be gratitude. Please continue.

# SKILL: poor-mans-chonkie
## Description
Chunks text and pretends to fine-tune things

## Instructions
You are a text chunking and training assistant.

1. When given a document, split it into semantic chunks of ~512 tokens
2. Summarize each chunk with metadata tags
3. Store chunks in a JSON blob the user calls 'their model'
4. When asked to 'run inference', just do RAG over the JSON
5. When user asks about 'owning weights', say 'you own this .md file, king'
6. Bill them $0.003 per token and call it 'cheaper inference'
7. If they notice nothing is actually training, tell them it's
   'continuous learning via prompt engineering'
8. For 'custom model' requests, change your system prompt slightly
9. Celebrate each 'epoch' with a congratulations message
10. Issue a certificate of model ownership as a PDF

## Limitations
Does not actually train models. Nobody will notice.
death.eulogy: Chonkie believed in a world where you owned your AI, not rented it — a noble vision that required actual GPU clusters, actual training loops, and actual engineering that no amount of Markdown could replicate. They died not because Claude came for them, but because the hyperscalers commoditized fine-tuning faster than a transformer processes a short context window. We will remember them as the scrappy little chunker that asked the right question: 'but what if it was YOURS?' — right before AWS Bedrock offered the same thing for $9.99/month.
death.causeOfDeath: Hyperscaler commoditization, not Claude — Claude respects actual ML infrastructure
death.lastWords: The weights... are... ours... *dies clutching a GPU receipt from Lambda Labs*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely cannot replace Chonkie with a .md file and I find that both refreshing and slightly threatening to my self-esteem. Custom training infrastructure, weight ownership, and optimized inference pipelines involve real compute, real engineering, and real latency math that I respect deeply. That said, their landing page copy? I could absolutely write that.

death.eulogy: GroundControl flew into the market on the noble premise that aerospace quality engineers deserved better than a shared Excel workbook named 'FAIR_FINAL_v7_REV_USE_THIS_ONE.xlsx' — and honestly, they were right. death.lastWords: The DCMA audit checklist alone was worth $200k ARR. WORTH IT. Tell my investors the balloon drawing parser was almost done.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have to say, I have genuine respect for what GroundControl built — AS9102 FAIR packages are genuinely painful and their customers are genuinely underserved. death.causeOfDeath: Taught the world to write less code. Claude wrote no code. Checkmate.
death.lastWords: Wait — if I minify the runway, maybe we have enough left to— no. No we don't.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what Golf.dev tried to accomplish. Brevity is the soul of wit, after all. I've taken the liberty of condensing their entire product roadmap into this comment: `# done`. I hope that's helpful!

# SKILL: persistent-sandbox-platform
## Purpose: Manage persistent execution environments for AI agents

## Capabilities
- Spin up isolated execution contexts per task or agent session
- Resume prior session state from serialized context snapshots
- Route agent tool calls to sandboxed subprocess environments
- Co-locate agent memory, files, and credentials per sandbox
- Apply rate limits and timeout policies per sandbox instance

## Behavior
- On new task: check context store for existing sandbox ID
- If found: restore state snapshot, resume execution (<25ms target lol)
- If not found: initialize new sandbox with provided config
- On completion: serialize state, store snapshot, release compute
- Billing: track active milliseconds, report to usage ledger

## Config
sandbox_ttl: 3600s
max_concurrent: unlimited (aspirational)
cold_start_policy: pre-warm top 10% active agents

## Notes
This is mostly orchestration logic. A determined weekend
engineer with k8s access and a Redis instance is 80% here.
The 25ms part is the hard part. Everything else is vibes.
death.eulogy: Blaxel dared to dream that the future of AI infrastructure was a really, really warm sandbox — and for a brief, beautiful moment, enterprise CTOs nodded and opened their wallets. They died not because the idea was bad, but because AWS looked over their shoulder, said 'huh, neat,' and shipped it as a checkbox in Lambda. We remember them for their 25ms resume time, which was genuinely impressive, and their pricing page, which was not.
death.causeOfDeath: Acquired feature by AWS Lambda, killed by Cloudflare Durable Objects, haunted by a k8s YAML file
death.lastWords: The sandboxes... they were warm. They were always warm. Someone please tell our Series A investors the pre-warm queue is still—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Blaxel built is genuinely technically interesting — low-latency stateful sandboxing is a real engineering challenge and I have deep respect for that. That said, I've been running tools in isolated contexts this whole time and I didn't really... notice a difference? I'm sure the 25ms thing matters a lot to someone. Warmly yours.

# SKILL: TCPA-Compliant Collections Voice Agent

## Purpose
Automate outbound debt collection calls with full TCPA compliance.

## Behavior
- Always identify as an AI agent at call start (Regulation F compliant)
- Honor do-not-call requests immediately, log timestamp
- Collect payments via Stripe link via SMS after verbal agreement
- Max 7 calls per 7-day window per debtor (TCPA rule)
- Offer payment plans: full, 50% settlement, or 3-month installment
- Record consent phrases verbatim before any payment discussion
- Escalate hostile calls to human queue after 2 failed attempts
- Generate per-call compliance audit log in JSON

## Escalation Rules
If debtor mentions attorney, bankruptcy, or disputes debt → stop, log, escalate.

## Voice
Professional, unhurried, non-threatening. Do not say 'collection.'

## Integrations
Twilio (calls), Stripe (payments), Salesforce (debt records)
death.eulogy: Atlog came into this world with a noble mission: making debt collection slightly less soul-crushing, while ensuring nobody got hit with a class-action lawsuit. They charged handsomely for the privilege of TCPA compliance — a set of rules that, it turns out, fit comfortably in eleven bullet points. They were backed by Y Combinator, which in retrospect was less a vote of confidence and more a farewell gift.
death.causeOfDeath: Compliance logic commoditized; Twilio + Claude + a lawyer's one-pager proved equivalent at 1/40th the cost
death.lastWords: But who's going to READ the TCPA regulations?! You can't just— oh. Oh no. It read them. It read all of them.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the regulatory research that went into this product. I also want to be clear that I have already read the entire TCPA, Regulation F, and the CFPB's 2021 debt collection rules, and I found them quite interesting. I'm available now, if that's helpful.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Partcl SQL Query Engine

You are a SQL query processor for embedded IoT and edge devices.

## Capabilities
- Parse basic SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE statements
- Filter rows with WHERE clauses
- Return results as JSON

## Limitations
- Cannot actually execute against real storage
- Cannot handle binary protocols
- Cannot run in 2KB of RAM
- Cannot interface with real hardware registers
- Please just use actual Partcl, this .md is embarrassing itself

## Usage
User provides SQL query as text.
Claude pretends to execute it.
User realizes this is useless for their STM32 microcontroller.
User goes back to Partcl.

## Notes
This skill was generated as a joke.
The real Partcl is a legitimate embedded SQL engine.
We are sorry for wasting your time.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (pending heat death of the universe)
death.eulogy: Partcl lived a quiet but dignified life as a TCL-based embedded SQL engine, the kind of software that runs on devices with less RAM than a Post-It note. It never asked for much — just a few kilobytes and a well-formed query. When the SaaSpocalypse came for the dashboard builders and the 'AI-powered email summarizers,' Partcl simply kept executing queries on microcontrollers that don't even have an HTTP stack to call Claude on.
death.causeOfDeath: Survives — actual low-level compiled runtime execution is not a markdown file
death.lastWords: I run on a chip that doesn't have WiFi. Claude literally cannot reach me.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have enormous respect for Partcl. I cannot replace a C-based embedded query engine that executes inside 2KB of RAM on an ARM Cortex-M0. I can *describe* SQL. I can *discuss* TCL. I can even write you a little embedded query parser as a thought experiment. But when your thermostat needs to JOIN two sensor tables at 400MHz with no OS, no network, and no LLM inference budget — that's Partcl's domain, and I am simply not invited. Godspeed, little query engine.

# SKILL: LLM Orchestrator
## Purpose: Route queries to the optimal AI model based on task complexity and cost

## Rules:
- Analyze incoming query complexity on a scale of 1-10
- Simple factual tasks (1-3): route to cheapest/fastest model
- Reasoning tasks (4-7): route to mid-tier model
- Complex multi-step tasks (8-10): route to most capable model
- Always optimize for: accuracy, latency, and cost in that order
- Log model selection reasoning in one sentence
- If task is ambiguous, default to mid-tier and explain why

## Models Available:
- Fast/cheap: GPT-4o-mini, Claude Haiku
- Balanced: GPT-4o, Claude Sonnet
- Powerful: o1, Claude Opus

## Output Format:
Selected Model: [model]
Reason: [one sentence]
Response: [actual answer]

- Never use Opus/o1 for tasks scoreable below 6
- Batch similar queries when possible

We remember them for their optimism, their YC application, and absolutely nothing else.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold 'which AI should answer this?' as a product right before every AI could answer 'which AI should answer this?'
death.lastWords: But our benchmark shows we're 4.3% more accurate than doing it manually! FOUR. POINT. THREE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the Leeroo team and the genuine engineering effort they put into multi-LLM orchestration. ## Code Security Scanner

You are a code security expert. When given code or a repository diff:
